questions.text: I notice when someone offers a carefully worded non-answer that sounds like an answer.
I resist productivity tools because they add overhead without matching how I naturally think.
I believe that my steady temperament reflects a genuine way of experiencing the world.
I feel most purposeful when I am tutoring, coaching, or guiding someone through a challenge.
I feel that the pull to respond to suffering — to give it genuine priority — is one of the most morally trustworthy impulses I experience.
I do not usually pick up on whether someone is genuinely enthusiastic or just being polite.
I feel that taking initiative without waiting for permission is a sign of strength.
I detect the subtle disappointment in someone's voice when they expected a different response.
I find meaning in being the person who gives the most and asks for the least.
I hold the view that the moral obligation to act in the face of suffering is not conditional on whether the action is easy, rewarded, or noticed.
I notice that I can re-enter a stressful situation after a break without the stress having compounded.
I notice when someone shifts from using 'I' to using 'we' to distribute responsibility for what they are about to say.
I hold the view that I deserve the rewards of my own hard work.
I instinctively protect my decision-making authority from interference.
I believe that the professionals who handle human suffering most effectively are those who treat it as a problem to solve rather than a burden to share.
I prefer that the group decides collectively how resources get distributed.
I compare my current progress to my original timeline to gauge whether I'm ahead or behind.
I find that my most productive days are those entirely devoted to my own agenda.
I can be in the middle of a crisis and still feel internally composed.
I experience the end of a vacation or a visit with someone I love as a sharp, almost physical loss.
I believe that building systems and routines is the smartest investment of time.
I experience the ending of a meaningful chapter — a project, a season, a relationship — as a kind of small death.
I believe that the ability to think clearly about human problems in the middle of emotional turmoil is rare and worth developing.
I am the person who advocates for more discussion when others want to rush a vote.
I feel that the best version of me is the one most devoted to others.
I have a tendency to offer help rather than take over when someone is struggling.
I feel that the moral claim of someone's suffering becomes more binding the more fully I understand their situation.
I keep promises and follow through even when it becomes inconvenient.
I do not naturally sense when it is my turn to speak versus when I should hold back.
I find that my emotional reactions compound — one small frustration on top of another can quickly become a meltdown.
I feel that cultivating personal expertise in my chosen field is a lifelong priority.
I find that analytical detachment during emotionally intense situations has given me the ability to evaluate multiple competing needs simultaneously without losing focus.
I gauge my productivity by how energized I feel, not by a tally of completed items.
I often end up being the one who holds others accountable for their commitments.
I approach problems by trying things and seeing what works rather than defining them first.
I do not automatically pick up on the difference between an invitation and a formality.
